questions.questionText: The biological indicator of fecal contamination of water is:
questions.optionB: E. coli (Escherichia coli) / coliform organisms — presence indicates fecal contamination; E. coli is the most specific indicator
questions.explanation: Core concept: E. coli = most reliable indicator of recent fecal contamination of water. Why correct: E. coli (thermotolerant coliform): exclusively fecal origin, abundant in human/animal feces, easily detectable by MPN/membrane filtration. WHO: E. coli should be ABSENT in drinking water. Total coliforms include environmental species (less specific). Why A wrong: C. perfringens spores indicate REMOTE contamination (persistent in environment — used as indicator of past pollution). Why C wrong: V. cholerae is a pathogen, not an indicator organism. Why D wrong: S. typhi is a pathogen. Clinical relevance: MPN (Most Probable Number): standard test for coliform count. BIS standard: 0 coliforms/100 mL in treated water. Horrock's apparatus: for chlorine dosing in the field. Exam trap: E. coli = recent fecal contamination. C. perfringens spores = remote/past contamination. Know the difference. Memory aid: 'E. coli = Each day's fEcal contamination Evidence.' Reference: Park – Environment.
questions.conceptTag: E. coli water fecal indicator MPN coliform

questions.questionText: Hardness of water is measured by:
questions.optionB: Calcium and magnesium salts content (total hardness = calcium + magnesium hardness; >200 mg/L = hard water — Clark's soap method)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Water hardness = Ca2+ and Mg2+ salt concentration. Why correct: Temporary hardness: Ca/Mg bicarbonates (removed by boiling). Permanent hardness: Ca/Mg sulfates/chlorides (removed by chemical treatment). Total hardness = temporary + permanent. Measurement: Clark's soap titration, EDTA titrimetric method. WHO: up to 200 mg/L acceptable; >300 mg/L = hard. Why A wrong: pH measures acidity/alkalinity (WHO: 6.5-8.5). Why C wrong: Chloride = indicates contamination (WHO: <250 mg/L). Why D wrong: Fluoride = dental effects (optimal 0.5-1.5 mg/L; >1.5 = fluorosis). Clinical relevance: Hard water: less lather with soap, scale in pipes. Soft water: may be more corrosive to pipes (leach metals). Exam trap: Fluoride >1.5 mg/L → dental fluorosis; >3 mg/L → skeletal fluorosis. Defluoridation: Nalgonda technique (alum + lime), activated alumina. Memory aid: 'Hard water = High Ca/Mg = Hard on soap.' Reference: Park – Water.
